name.text: Week 16 – Q5: 5G Link Budget and Friis Equation

questiontext.text:
Scenario: You are a network planning engineer deploying a new 5G base station on a university campus. The base station operates at \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz and must provide reliable coverage to mobile phones at a distance of \(r = {@r_m@}\) m.

Your task: calculate the expected received signal strength and determine whether the link has sufficient margin for reliable communication.

System parameters:

  • Transmit power: \(P_t = {@P_t@}\) dBm
  • Transmit antenna gain: \(G_t = {@G_t@}\) dBi
  • Receive antenna gain: \(G_r = {@G_r@}\) dBi

Part A — Link Budget Calculation

(a) Calculate the free-space wavelength \(\lambda\).

\(\lambda = \) [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] m

Enter in metres, e.g., 0.234.

(b) Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB.

\[\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi r}{\lambda}\right)\]

FSPL = [[input:ans2]] [[validation:ans2]] dB

Enter in dB, e.g., 17.5.

(c) Using the Friis transmission equation, calculate the received power.

\[P_r\text{ (dBm)} = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}\]

\(P_r = \) [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] dBm

Enter in dBm (can be negative), e.g., -55.

Part B — Distance and Frequency Effects

(d) A student moves twice as far from the base station (to \({@2*r_m@}\) m). By how many dB does the received power change?

\(\Delta P_r = \) [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] dB

Enter in dB (negative means decrease), e.g., -17.5.

(e) In a 5G mmWave system, the base station uses a 64-element phased array to compensate for high path loss. Which course modules are involved in the full system design?

[[input:ans5]] [[validation:ans5]]

(f) If the operating frequency were increased (e.g., from 3.5 GHz to 28 GHz) while keeping distance, transmit power, and antenna gains the same, what would happen to the received power?

[[input:ans6]] [[validation:ans6]]

Part C — Coverage Assessment

(g) A mobile phone requires a minimum received power of {@sensitivity@} dBm for a reliable {@if is(f_GHz <= 6) then "sub-6 GHz" else "mmWave"@} connection. What is the link margin (how many dB above the minimum)?

Link margin = \(P_r - P_{r,\min} = \) [[input:ans7]] [[validation:ans7]] dB

Enter in dB, e.g., 17.5.

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Given: \(f = {@f_GHz@}\) GHz, \(r = {@r_m@}\) m, \(P_t = {@P_t@}\) dBm, \(G_t = {@G_t@}\) dBi, \(G_r = {@G_r@}\) dBi.

(a) Wavelength:

\[\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{{@f_Hz@}} = {@tans1@} \text{ m}\]

(b) Free-space path loss:

\[\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times {@r_m@}}{{@tans1@}}\right) = 20\log_{10}({@float(4*%pi*r_m/tans1)@}) = {@tans2@} \text{ dB}\]

(c) Received power (Friis equation):

\[P_r = {@P_t@} + {@G_t@} + {@G_r@} - {@tans2@} = {@tans3@} \text{ dBm}\]

(d) Effect of doubling distance:

FSPL contains \(20\log_{10}(r)\). If \(r \to 2r\):

\[\Delta\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6.02 \text{ dB}\]

So received power decreases by 6 dB (factor of 4 in power). This is the inverse-square law: \(P \propto 1/r^2\).

(e) Cross-module connections in 5G:

  • M1 (Circuits): Bias circuits, power management for the transceiver
  • M2 (Laplace/Transients): Switching amplifier transient response
  • M3 (Magnetics): On-chip inductors for impedance matching
  • M4 (Waves/Radiation): Antenna arrays, beam steering, wave propagation — at 28 GHz, even PCB traces are transmission lines

Answer: All four modules (M1 + M2 + M3 + M4) are required.

(f) Higher frequency means shorter \(\lambda\). Since FSPL = \(20\log_{10}(4\pi r/\lambda)\), a shorter \(\lambda\) increases the argument, increasing path loss and thus reducing received power. This is why mmWave 5G (28-39 GHz) suffers much higher path loss than sub-6 GHz bands and requires high-gain phased arrays to compensate.

(g) Link margin:

\[\text{Margin} = P_r - P_{r,\min} = {@tans3@} - ({@sensitivity@}) = {@tans6@} \text{ dB}\]

{@if is(tans6 > 20) then "This link has a comfortable margin. In practice, engineers aim for 10-20 dB of margin to account for fading, rain attenuation, and building penetration losses." else "This margin is relatively thin. In real deployments, fading, rain, and building penetration could reduce the signal further, potentially causing outages."@}

questionnote.text: {@sconcat("f_GHz=", f_GHz, " r=", r_m, " Pt=", P_t, " Gt=", G_t, " Gr=", G_r, " Pr=", tans3, " margin=", tans6)@}

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. \(\lambda = c/f\). Convert GHz to Hz: {@f_GHz@} GHz = {@f_Hz@} Hz.

feedbackvariables.text: err_10log_flag: if abs(ans2 - FSPL_err)/abs(FSPL_err) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You used \(10\log_{10}\) instead of \(20\log_{10}\). FSPL uses \(20\log_{10}\) because it originates from a power ratio involving \((r/\lambda)^2\): \(20\log_{10}(x) = 10\log_{10}(x^2)\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. FSPL = \(20\log_{10}(4\pi r/\lambda)\). Check your \(\lambda\) from part (a).

feedbackvariables.text: err_add_flag: if abs(ans3 - Pr_err_add)/abs(Pr_err_add) < 0.06 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: You added FSPL instead of subtracting it. Path loss represents energy lost during propagation, so it must be subtracted: \(P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Friis equation: \(P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL}\). All values are in dB/dBm, so use addition and subtraction.

feedbackvariables.text: err_3dB_flag: if abs(ans4 - delta_err)/abs(delta_err) < 0.1 then 1 else 0;

truefeedback.text: Correct. Doubling the distance increases FSPL by \(20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6\) dB, so \(P_r\) drops by 6 dB.
You said \(-3\) dB, which would be halving the power (doubling the distance in a \(1/r\) system). But EM power decays as \(1/r^2\), so doubling \(r\) gives \(1/4\) power = \(-6\) dB.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. FSPL contains \(20\log_{10}(r)\). Doubling \(r\): \(\Delta\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6\) dB. So \(P_r\) decreases by about 6 dB.

truefeedback.text: Correct. A 5G mmWave system requires all four modules: bias circuits (M1), transient response (M2), on-chip inductors for matching (M3), and antenna arrays + propagation (M4).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. At mmWave frequencies, even PCB traces become transmission lines (M4). The system also needs bias circuits (M1), switching amplifiers with transient response (M2), and on-chip inductors for impedance matching (M3). All four modules are involved.

truefeedback.text: Correct. Higher frequency = shorter \(\lambda\) = higher FSPL = lower received power. This is why mmWave 5G needs high-gain antennas to compensate.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Look at the FSPL formula: \(20\log_{10}(4\pi r/\lambda)\). If frequency increases, \(\lambda\) decreases, making \(4\pi r/\lambda\) larger, which increases FSPL — so received power decreases.

feedbackvariables.text: /* Follow-through from student's own Pr */ ft_margin: float(ans3 - sensitivity);

truefeedback.text: Correct. The link has {@tans6@} dB of margin above the minimum sensitivity.
Correct method (\(P_r - P_{r,\min}\)), but your \(P_r\) from part (c) was not correct. Half credit for correct follow-through.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Link margin = \(P_r - P_{r,\min}\). Use your \(P_r\) from part (c) and the sensitivity threshold of {@sensitivity@} dBm.

hint.text: Link budget basics: Start with \(\lambda = c/f\) (convert GHz to Hz). FSPL uses \(20\log_{10}\), not \(10\log_{10}\) — this is because the power ratio \((4\pi r/\lambda)^2\) is inside the log, and \(10\log_{10}(x^2) = 20\log_{10}(x)\). In the Friis equation, all quantities are in dB/dBm, so gains are added and path loss is subtracted.

Distance and frequency effects: When distance doubles, FSPL increases by \(20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6\) dB. This is the inverse-square law: power decays as \(1/r^2\), so doubling \(r\) means \(1/4\) power = \(-6\) dB (not \(-3\) dB). For the frequency question: look at where \(\lambda\) appears in the FSPL formula — shorter wavelength means higher loss.

Coverage assessment: Link margin = received power minus minimum sensitivity. This tells you how much buffer exists before the signal becomes unreliable. In real deployments, engineers need 10-20 dB of margin to account for fading, rain, and building penetration.
